speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date, October 25, 1996. Survivor, Erwin Chaim. Interviewer, Terri Auerbach, T-E-R-R-I A-U-E-R-B-A-C-H. Denver, Colorado, United States of America. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is October 25, 1996. My name's Terri Auerbach, T-E-R-R-I A-U-E-R-B-A-C-H. And it's my honor to be interviewing Mr. Erwin Chaim in Denver, Colorado, United States of America, and we're doing this in English. OK. Why don't we begin first with you telling me your name and spelling your name the way you were given it when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Erwin Chaim, E-R-W-I-N, Chaim, C-H-A-I-M, pronounced Chaim. In Germany, it was 'khime.' In Jewish, it's 'hi-am,' 'to life.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me your birth date.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1928, the 23rd of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which makes you how old today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 68.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me the city and country you were born in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berlin, Germany, right in the middle of the city, close to Alexanderplatz, which is the center of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into some other of the facts that we will do, I know you wanted to read a little portion of this book. So why don't you do that now, and then we'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. By a coincident, I just started reading and finished it. This book, a rather heavy book, called by Claire Rayner. And it kind of starts and describes the Jewish history, starting with the destruction of the Second Temple in the year 70 of the Common Era. And she kind of sums up the instructions, or rather the beginning of these two families. 'And so it was that the wandering began. It would be almost another 2,000 years before some of the descendants of Tamar and Susannah, by then split into many family lines, would meet again in the remote, cold, northern island of England, as three tributaries of the main river of the wandering Jew. 2,000 years or fear and hunger, as well as peace and plenty. 2,000 Years of stubbornly holding on to their faith, however painful non-believers made that devotion. 2,000 years of suffering, of striving, and above all, of survival.' And now, here I am, 2,000 years later, 1996, telling my story of our survival, of escaping out of Nazi Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you. Let's-- let's begin with your family now. Why don't you tell me the name of your father and spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Henry Chaim, Heinrich in Germany. Henry actually changes kind of to Henry here. Heinrich, H-E-I-N-R-I-C-H, Heinrich Chaim, 'khime.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a dentist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any special memories you'd like to tell me about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was a very pleasant man. He married my mother in 1927, and I came along in 1928. And he also joined the army in the First World War. He was wounded and lost his hearing in one ear. And as an aside, I was named after one of my mother's brothers, Erwin, who died in the First World War in the Battle of Verdun in France. My good uncle, Hanz Palnitzky, also was a soldier in the first World War. He was an officer. And he was buried alive for a number of hours. And when they dug him up, he was a raving maniac and spent quite a bit of time in-- in-- in a hospital and always afterwards had claustrophobia. The reason I'm bringing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh boy. Hanz, H-A-N-Z, Palznitz, P-A-L-Z-N-I-T-Z, Palznitz, I think. All of these people were in the German army, fighting for Germany, because we were Germans of a Jewish faith. This was our fatherland. And that's what made it so hard to believe that a guy like Hitler-- Hitler isn't going to go away. He isn't going to do what he did. We are Germans. This is why a lot of people just never thought about leaving Germany. Besides the fact that it was very hard to-- you had a business. You had a good living. You spoke the language. You go to different country, you don't speak the language. You don't have a job. And he's going to go away. A lot of people believed that. My father woke up to the fact that he isn't going to go away. Things are going to get worse. In about 1930, 1932, he tried at that time-- he was just a beginning dentist. He didn't have too much money, really, at the time. He tried at that time to go to leave Germany and to go to Palestine with a physician. They put a down payment on a truck and a trailer, outfitted it like a dental and a physician's practice, and also pulled a trailer as living quarters. It didn't work out. The physician got cold feet, dropped out. My father lost whatever he put down on that particular venture. He kept trying to get out of Germany several times, any country that would take him. The unfortunate thing was that at that time, doors of other countries began to close. The quotas, which were quite generous at one time, began to drop drastically. [INAUDIBLE] You just could not get any permission to go into these countries, be they England, be they United States, be they Australia, be they anything. It became harder and harder. On top of that, then there was a black market building up of trying to get visas, permission, exit visas, entrance visas, all those things. And it turned out to be bogus. My father again got some bogus visas which then were not accepted, lost more money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was all between 1931, '32, clear into 1938, when we finally got permission, with a ridiculously low price, five US dollars at the time, to come to Shanghai, China, Shanghai, China, the part that was occupied by the Japanese government.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And before we get to that, let's backtrack--

speaker: interviewee
text: Back.

speaker: interviewer
text: --just a little bit. Tell me about-- tell me about your mother. Tell me her name, and spell her name for me too.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Else, E-L-S-E, again Palnitzky, married my father, Chaim, 'khime.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were both your parents from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both my parents were from Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they got married in Berlin in 1927. And again, I came along in 1928. [LAUGHS] And I do have a sister too, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came along in 1930, two years younger than I am. And her name is Evelyne, E-V-E-L-Y-N-E, Evelyne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother was kind of a socialite, really. She worked also in my-- I just remembered. My mother was very close to her brother, Hanz Palnitzky. The reason was that my brother-- no, her brother had a very special business. He made shirts to order to all-- literally, really, all the famous people and actors in Berlin. And he started that business when he found that when his father, Hanz Palnitzky's father, who also was in the shirt business, but he made the shirts wholesale. And my uncle decided that was not good enough for him. He wanted to have contact with the clientele himself. Therefore, he started making shirts to order. And of course, like any new business, had trouble making ends meet, but pretty soon became quite famous in Berlin. Well, my mother's dowry became part of the business venture. And so my mother had a part-time interest, I guess, in-- in-- in holding the business together. And she became, I guess, the bookkeeper. So she did that. And she also, of course, did my father's books too. She was good at that. She helped in the business there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any special memories of you with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have some special memories. Unfortunately, they are really not very pleasant. What happened was that my father, like I said before, tried to get out of Germany in the worst way, and of course into the best country. The best country was the USA, which at that time, we were not able to get to. Second choice, I think, was England. We couldn't get there either. So we tried other countries which I didn't know about or can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But finally, starting in 1939, '38, '39, we were thinkin' of going to China. Well, my mother did not agree with that at all. 'What? Go to China? This is the end of the world. China. Impossible. I'm not going to go.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember very well what happened when my father finally got the permission to go to China. My mother became hysterical. My father had to lock her up in my-- children's room, I guess, nursery. She started banging on the door with my favorite toy, an electrified motor boat, which I used in the bathtub [LAUGHS] at the time. And she banged and banged on the door and ruined my motor boat. Kind of [LAUGHS] one of the memories that I have. To jump ahead just a little bit, when we did get to China, unfortunately, in the first week that we got there, a typhoon hit. We had temporary quarters at the time on the main floor of a Chinese house. It rained. It rained. Drainage was very bad, and it flooded the floor to about one foot of water, yellow, stinking river water. And my mother just about died right there and then. And she never-- she never, never really got over that. The dirt, the filth, the heat, and the bugs, bedbugs, mosquitoes, cockroaches by the thousands. She did not-- she did not like-- none of us liked Shanghai, but of course it saved our lives. But she really suffered the most, I think. She did get yellow fever, by the way. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: In Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me, jaundice. In Shanghai, yeah, nearly died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your life obviously took a turn in the conditions you were living in in Shanghai. What were-- tell me about growing up, what your home was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Growing up. Growing up in-- in-- in Germany now, in Berlin. Well, OK--

speaker: interviewer
text: And describe your home for me. Do you remember your address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I was-- I spent my first two years at Dircksenstrasse. This was with my grandparents. And my grandparents lived at Dircksenstrasse, which was a manufacturing district, right across from the S-Bahn. This is the elevated trains. And I guess my hobby started right there and then. I still like trains. I still like the electric trains, because that's what I was exposed to on the balcony practically every day. We then moved away from that, only about four blocks away to Bruckenstrasse, [INAUDIBLE], which was on the Jannowitzbrucke, at the Jannowitzbrucke.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jannowitzbrucke is just a station stop of the elevated trains. That is one station stop away from Alexanderplatz, which is the center of Berlin. So we lived in a rather exciting part of town. The Bruckenstrasse was famous because there were two [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] pleasure boat stations that left from Jannowitzbrucke to go to the different lakes which were all around Berlin. Berlin is very famous for all their wooded areas, for their rivers, and their seas, their lakes. Also, there was a famous park just two blocks away from Bruckenstrasse, which had a small exhibit of live bears. Because Berlin-- bears, 'bear-lin,' that was their-- their mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 2 to 4 million people. It was a huge city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very large too. Very large. Very large. We had our own social activities. We had our own theaters. We had, of course, all the synagogues, schools. I went to Jewish schools. They were separate. And speaking of schools, I started going to the school at Kaiserstrasse, which again, I walked to. It was roughly, oh, maybe between 10 and 20 blocks, which a lot of people walked in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Went to that school. And then pretty soon, the Hitler part of the Nazis started closing the schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you say that-- I don't mean to interrupt you here, but I just wanted to get the names of your grandparents also. Or at least, what you said-- did you live with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same home. That was my grandmother. I called her Omi. Her name was Friederike. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Don't ask me to spell that. And my grandfather-- which I never met-- my grandfather met before I was born, died of tuberculosis. Heavy smoker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have memories of your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My grandfather's name was William. My-- my grandmother, yes, I spent a lot of time with my grandmother. Matter of fact, I spent a lot of time with my uncle, Uncle Hanz, and my grandmother in their-- in their home, because for some reason or another, my uncle took care of me a little bit more than my parents did. Because my uncle had this famous business of making shirts to order, he was more protected than my father was. My father lost his practice. He wasn't able to practice right after 1934, so he started working illegally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, as a dentist, and behind closed doors. And so I think the idea was to get me out of the house as much as possible. This is speculation. I don't really know this for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your dad did have his practice, did he-- did people come to him, or did he go to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, no. They came to him. He had a regular practice. The custom in Europe is that you work where you live, or you live where you work. So the front part of the house was the operatory and a waiting room and a laboratory. We had a rather elaborate apartment on the second floor, which consisted of 12, 12 rooms. We also had a maid, and she had a room by herself too. In 1933, '34, or '35, somewhere around that, the apartment was divided in half. We were not allowed to have a 12-room apartment. Luckily enough, we were able to keep the front part, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know how that happened. Instead of being in the back, we were able to be in the front, which was, of course, the better part of it. So that meant that the laboratory became the kitchen. The operatory had in it the operatory and the laboratory. The laboratory is where you make the teeth. My father made their own teeth. And the waiting room became the bedroom where we slept. And the dining room became the waiting room, [LAUGHS] so it became rather compact. At that time also, my-- our maid and nurse, I guess, which was with us since we were born--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Her name was Minna Strensky. She was actually the maid of my grandmother, Omi, and she also brought up my mother. And then when my mother married, she was given more or less, so to speak, to my mother to bring up their kids. So she was part of the family. And when in 1935, she was not allowed to work for Jews anymore-- she was not Jewish. She was a Gentile, Christian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She didn't know what to do. Her life was with us. We were her family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think her parents were farmers. And she was just another girl that-- another mouth to feed, and those were hired out. That was very common in those days. And she became part of our family. When she had to leave, this was-- this was-- this was very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she wasn't allowed to come in your home at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she visit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She-- she lived with us anyway. She lived. And she had to leave. What happened to her, I don't know. I have no recollection at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about your friends and school. Did you-- And your-- let's do school and friends, then I have another question.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. School and friends. Well, like I said before, school for me was a chore. I was not a very fast learner. And when it became apparent that the school capacity, which maybe at one time was like maybe 10, 15, 20 pupils, became 75 and 100, you just didn't learn anything. So my parents realized that and took me out of the first school, Kaiserstrasse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say that was a Jewish school or a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. It was a Jewish-- Jewish-- Jewish public school. They then enrolled me in a school in Pankow, P-A-N-K-O-W, a suburb of Berlin, which was an orphanage. It took quite a bit of pull to get me into this orphanage. Because the classes there was like maybe 5 to 10 people in a class, kids in a class. I stayed there and lived there. And I was very unhappy, by the way. I could not get used to that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was it from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was a suburb of Berlin, and I would say a half an hour by S-Bahn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about six and seven years old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister also there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My sister-- my sister stayed in her school. They-- the school system was divided between boys and girls. And my sister stayed in the-- in the public Jewish school, despite the fact that it became larger and larger. But my sister learned [LAUGHS] much better than I did. Well, what happened was, in a very short time, I would say a half a year, the Jewish school in the suburb, Pankow, got closed. And all the Jewish kids had to come to the same Waisenhaus orphanage. Boom. Here we go again. 50, maybe 30 people, I don't remember. When that happened, my-- my parents threw up their arms and said, OK, it is time for both myself and Evelyne to go to a private school, the Kaliski School, which was owned by an American, therefore could not be tampered with. The Kaliski School was in the suburbs of Berlin, Dahlem, D-A-H-L-E-M, which I reach by subway train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived at home for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived at home. I left in the morning by subway, about a 45-minute ride. Stayed there all day, had lunch and an afternoon snack there, and then left the school-- with my sister, by the way, we both enrolled at the same time-- at about, oh, I don't know, 5 or 6 o'clock at night, during rush hour, where it was-- was much safer to take public transportation, not be subjected to any indignitaries.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was all the way until we left Germany in 1940. And we enrolled, I would say, in about 1938. We went there for two, two and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of things did you notice changing? I mean, you said, so you weren't subject to anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I was beaten up twice by the Hitler Youth. Once, the first time, in the park that I mentioned before, the [GERMAN] park, which was around the corner from us, where the bears were. And I was just, you know, fell upon by these, because why? Because I was a Jew. That's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do they know you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't exactly look Aryan. I don't have blond hair. I don't have a short nose. And at that time now, we are talking 1935, we did not have to wear any identification marks, no star or anything like that. I guess I looked Jewish, and therefore, they beat me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone around try and help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no-- no. No-- nobody helped me. I guess-- I-- I really don't remember anymore. I just remember I was beaten up. And basically I didn't have any broken bones.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about maybe eight, eight, nine. The second time, one person, one kid, one guy, OK? And he was in uniform. He had the uniform, the Hitler uniform on. Tried to beat me up. I ran. He ran after me. I ran home. He followed me. And he beat me up right in front of my parents' front door. Well, when they heard the commotion going on, they then opened the door and rescued me, so to speak. That's the only two times that this happened to me. But that was enough for my parents to realize that riding my bicycle on the streets and going to the park alone was not a very good thing to do. After-- just to jump ahead here a little bit-- after the practice in Buchau, which my father had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's--

speaker: interviewee
text: --was taken away by the Nazis too in 1935--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Buchau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buchau is a suburb of Germany, about 50 miles away. My father had a second practice over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got there by car, by automobile. My father had a very small automobile, a three-seater, one-cylinder car with one headlight and one door. Basically speaking, a covered motorcycle in a way. But to come back to that, rather than being in the parks, being on the street in the summertime during vacation, I had a season ticket for the Berliner Zoo. And I spent a lot of time in the zoo. I don't remember if I was with my sister or not. This was a safe haven, so to speak, so I played in the zoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop the tape right here, and we'll change tapes.
